option: Cafe/restaurant: warm earth palette + editorial food photography + asymmetric menu grid
best_for: Local cafes bakeries and restaurants wanting an inviting non-chain feel that still reads premium
avoid_when: A fast-casual chain wanting a clean high-volume ordering flow where warmth would slow conversion
tradeoffs: Builds appetite appeal and locality fast; genuinely good food photography is a real production cost not a template swap

option: Healthcare/clinic: muted sage-and-clay or cool-clinical palette + generous whitespace + calm serif headings
best_for: Clinics wellness practices and healthcare providers needing to signal trust and calm without looking cold or corporate
avoid_when: Emergency or urgent-care contexts where speed-to-action beats calm aesthetics
tradeoffs: Reduces the sterile hospital-brochure look competitors default to; whitespace discipline requires real content editing not just CSS

option: Legal/law firm: deep ink palette + serif display type + structured grid signaling authority
best_for: Law firms and professional services wanting gravitas and credibility over playful branding
avoid_when: A boutique or consumer-facing legal service wanting approachability over authority
tradeoffs: Signals seriousness and competence fast; can tip into generic-corporate if the type pairing isn't deliberately chosen (see UX024)

option: SaaS/tech: bold accent-on-neutral + product screenshots as hero content + confident type scale
best_for: B2B and B2C SaaS wanting the product itself to be the primary visual proof not illustration
avoid_when: Pre-product-market-fit landing pages with nothing real to screenshot yet
tradeoffs: Product-as-hero builds credibility fastest for software; requires the actual product UI to already look good

option: E-commerce/retail: high-contrast product photography grid + urgency-aware color blocking
best_for: Retail and e-commerce wanting fast scannability and clear purchase-path visual hierarchy
avoid_when: Luxury retail where urgency tactics undercut the premium positioning (use UX007's jewel-tone approach instead)
tradeoffs: Optimizes for conversion-path clarity; can read generic-Shopify if the grid and color-blocking aren't customized past the template defaults

option: Real estate: large-format imagery + minimal chrome + map-integrated browsing
best_for: Real estate and property listings where the inventory itself is the visual content
avoid_when: A real-estate SaaS tool for agents where the product not listings is the hero (use UX071 instead)
tradeoffs: Lets genuinely good property photography carry the design; weak photography has nowhere to hide in this pattern

option: Fitness/wellness: high-energy duotone or gradient + dynamic diagonal layout + bold sans display type
best_for: Gyms fitness apps and wellness brands wanting motion and energy conveyed even in static layouts
avoid_when: A clinical wellness/medical-adjacent brand wanting calm over energy (use UX069 instead)
tradeoffs: Conveys momentum and motivation visually; diagonal/dynamic layouts need real design discipline to stay legible not chaotic

option: Finance/fintech: single-hue data-dense palette + tabular numeric precision + restrained motion
best_for: Fintech and financial services needing to convey precision and trust through information design itself
avoid_when: A consumer fintech onboarding flow wanting warmth and approachability over pure data precision
tradeoffs: Precision-as-aesthetic differentiates from both stuffy legacy-bank and overly playful neobank extremes; requires real data design discipline (tabular figures UX030 numeric alignment)

option: Education/e-learning: friendly rounded type + progress-forward layout + optimistic color use
best_for: Online courses schools and e-learning platforms wanting approachable, achievement-oriented design
avoid_when: Elite/prestige-positioned academic institutions wanting gravitas over approachability (lean toward UX070's authority pattern instead)
tradeoffs: Lowers the intimidation barrier to starting and completing courses; can read juvenile if rounded/optimistic choices aren't balanced with real typographic discipline

option: Nonprofit/cause-driven: authentic documentary photography + restrained single-accent palette + narrative-first layout
best_for: Nonprofits and cause-driven organizations needing to build trust and emotional connection without looking exploitative
avoid_when: Any context where the cause itself should not be visually dramatized for effect
tradeoffs: Authenticity over polish builds more durable trust in this category than production value; stock-photo shortcuts are especially visible and damaging here

option: Salon/beauty/personal care: soft warm-neutral palette + generous whitespace + before/after or portfolio imagery as hero
best_for: Local salons, barbershops, spas, and aesthetics clinics where the buying decision is visual trust plus booking convenience.
avoid_when: A med-spa or clinical-adjacent service that needs to read as clinically credible -- pull toward the Healthcare/clinic direction (UX069) instead of over-softening it.
tradeoffs: Warm/soft palette builds approachability fast, but reads as a generic 'beauty site' without a distinct type pairing or a real portfolio -- stock photography is more visible/damaging here than in most other sectors, invest in real work photos.

option: Trades/home services (contractor, plumber, electrician, cleaning): high-trust utilitarian palette + before/after or job-site photography + prominent phone/quote CTA
best_for: Local service businesses booked by phone or quote request more than self-serve checkout, where trust and responsiveness matter more than visual flourish.
avoid_when: The business is premium/design-forward positioning (e.g. high-end renovation, architecture) -- that calls for the Real estate direction's (UX073) editorial restraint instead.
tradeoffs: Utilitarian directness converts well for urgent, local-intent searches, but leaning too utilitarian can make an actually-differentiated premium provider look like every competitor -- confirm positioning before defaulting here.

option: Events/weddings/photography: full-bleed editorial imagery + minimal chrome + serif or script accent type for emotional warmth
best_for: Portfolio-driven creative services (wedding planners, photographers, videographers, event venues) where the work itself is the primary sales asset.
avoid_when: The imagery library is thin or inconsistent in quality -- a sparse, low-quality gallery reads worse under a full-bleed treatment than under a smaller-image grid; don't over-commit to a format the content can't fill.
tradeoffs: Maximal visual impact when the portfolio is strong, but this direction lives or dies on image quality and consistency more than any other sector here.

option: Consulting/professional services: confident neutral palette + credential-forward layout (case studies, logos, results) + direct-response CTA structure
best_for: B2B consultants, agencies, coaches, and advisors selling expertise or outcomes rather than a tangible product, where trust signals do the persuasion work.
avoid_when: The business is genuinely product-led (a SaaS tool, not advisory services) -- that's the SaaS/tech direction (UX071) instead, which leans on the product itself as proof rather than case studies.
tradeoffs: Credential-forward layouts build trust fast for high-consideration B2B purchases, but overdoing logo walls or testimonials without real specificity reads as generic-agency filler -- insist on named, specific results.

option: Pet care/veterinary: warm-approachable palette (not clinical-cold) + candid animal photography + clear service/pricing structure
best_for: Veterinary clinics, groomers, pet sitters, and boarding/daycare businesses -- the emotional bond with the animal is the actual product, don't treat it like generic Healthcare/clinic (UX069).
avoid_when: It's a large multi-location veterinary hospital brand needing a more clinical-credible direction -- pull toward Healthcare/clinic (UX069) at that scale instead of staying purely warm/approachable.
tradeoffs: Warm, candid photography builds emotional trust fast for small local practices, but a hospital-scale operation needs some clinical-credibility signal blended in or it reads as under-serious for a medical service.

option: Hospitality/boutique lodging (B&B, boutique hotel, short-term rental): immersive full-bleed environment photography + minimal booking-forward chrome
best_for: Boutique hotels, B&Bs, vacation rentals, and small hospitality brands selling an experience or atmosphere as much as a room.
avoid_when: It's a large multi-property hotel chain needing rate/availability search as the primary interaction -- that's a booking-engine-first product need, not an art-direction one; this direction is for the smaller, atmosphere-led end of hospitality.
tradeoffs: Immersive photography sells the experience effectively, but needs a genuinely strong location/interior photo set -- weak photography undermines this direction more visibly than a more conservative layout would.

option: Auto services (repair, detailing, dealership): bold high-contrast palette + technical/mechanical imagery + trust badges (certifications, warranty) prominent
best_for: Auto repair shops, detailing services, and independent dealerships where technical credibility and price transparency drive the decision.
avoid_when: It's a luxury/exotic dealership positioning on prestige rather than value -- that leans toward the luxury-minimal style vocabulary entry (UX143) and a more restrained palette instead of bold/high-contrast.
tradeoffs: Bold contrast and visible certifications build fast technical trust for commodity auto services, but the same treatment undersells a luxury dealership's actual positioning -- confirm which one applies before defaulting to bold.
